A score of 0.73 indicates strong similarity - these vectors are pointing in quite similar directions.

NOTE : Cosine similarity gives scores from -1 to +1, allowing you to rank results by relevance - highest scores = best matches.

Read the entire series here :

  • Part I - Why Cosine Similarity > L2 Distance for RAG Systems (usually) [Part 1/3]
  • Part II - How to Actually Interpret L2 Distance Results [Part 2/3]
  • Part III - The Production Reality: Why Cosine Similarity Often Wins Anyway [Part 3/3]

 

  •

entry.title: How to Actually Interpret L2 Distance Results [Part 2/3]
entry.content:
In my previous post on choosing between Cosine Similarity and L2 Distance for RAG systems, I focused on when to use each metric. But there's a critical issue I didn't address: how to interpret L2 Distance results.

Here's the problem: while Cosine Similarity gives you a clean -1 to +1 range, L2 Distance ranges from 0 to infinity—making it nearly impossible to judge similarity without proper context.

The Interpretation Challenge:

Using the same vectors from my previous post:

  • Vector A = [3, 4, 0]
  • Vector B = [1, 2, 2]

We calculated Cosine Similarity = 0.73 (strong similarity), but what about L2 Distance?

L2 Distance Calculation:

L2 Distance = √[(a₁ - b₁)² + (a₂ - b₂)² + (a₃ - b₃)²]
L2 Distance = √[(3-1)² + (4-2)² + (0-2)²] = √(4 + 4 + 4) = 3.46

But here's the question: Is 3.46 similar or different? The answer depends entirely on your data range.

Real-world context matters:

  • If your vectors represent pixel values (0-255 range): 3.46 = very similar
  • If they represent financial data in millions: 3.46 = practically identical
  • If they represent coordinates in a 0-50 range: need to calculate to know

The Solution: Normalize by Maximum Possible Distance

For our example in a 0-50 range:

  • Most different possible vectors: [0,0,0] and [50,50,50]
  • Maximum L2 Distance: √[(50-0)² + (50-0)² + (50-0)²] = 86.6
  • Relative distance: 3.46 ÷ 86.6 = 0.04 (4% of maximum distance)
  • Verdict: Very similar!

Universal Normalization Formula:

For any vector space with data ranging from min to max:

Maximum L2 Distance = √[d × (max - min)²]

Where d = number of dimensions (3 for our example)

Relative Distance = Actual L2 Distance ÷ Maximum L2 Distance

Quick Reference Scale:

  • 0-6% of max distance: Very similar
  • 6-17%: Similar
  • 17-35%: Moderately different
  • 35%+: Very different

Pro tip: This normalization gives you a 0-100% similarity scale that works regardless of your data range—making L2 Distance as interpretable as Cosine Similarity.

Next week: I'll cover what happens when you can't determine your maximum distance, and why this leads many production systems back to Cosine Similarity.

Read the entire series here :

  • Part I - Why Cosine Similarity > L2 Distance for RAG Systems (usually) [Part 1/3]
  • Part II - How to Actually Interpret L2 Distance Results [Part 2/3]
  • Part III - The Production Reality: Why Cosine Similarity Often Wins Anyway [Part 3/3]

entry.title: The Production Reality: Why Cosine Similarity Often Wins Anyway [Part 3/3]
entry.content:
In Part 2, I showed how to normalize L2 Distance for proper interpretation. But here's what happens when theory meets production reality.

The Challenge: When You Can't Determine Maximum Distance

While we always know our vector dimensions (384, 768, 1536, etc.), normalization still faces real-world challenges:

Problem 1: Theoretical vs. Actual Range

Example with a hypothetical model:

  • Model docs say: "768-dimensional vectors, values range [-1, 1]"
  • You calculate: Max L2 = √[768 × (1-(-1))²] = √[768 × 4] = 55.4
  • Reality check: Your actual embeddings range [-0.4, 0.3]
  • Actual max L2: √[768 × (0.3-(-0.4))²] = √[768 × 0.49] = 19.4
  • Your error: Using 55.4 instead of 19.4 = off by 185%!

Problem 2: Dynamic Datasets

  • New documents added daily → empirical maximum keeps changing
  • Document duplicates → many L2 distances = 0, skewing statistics
  • Content types evolve → data ranges shift over time

The Modern Model Advantage: Unit Vectors

Here's where it gets interesting. Many modern embedding models (including OpenAI) solve this by outputting unit vectors—vectors with magnitude = 1.

Unit vector example:

  • Vector A = [0.6, 0.8, 0]
  • Vector B = [0.8, 0.6, 0]
  • Check: |A| = √(0.6² + 0.8² + 0²) = √1 = 1 ✓ (unit vector)

For unit vectors:

  • Maximum L2 distance is always 2 (between opposite vectors like [1,0,0] and [-1,0,0])
  • L2 Distance = √[(0.6-0.8)² + (0.8-0.6)² + (0-0)²] = √(0.04 + 0.04) = 0.28
  • Normalization: 0.28 ÷ 2 = 0.14 = 14% of maximum = very similar

Comparing approaches with the same vectors:

  • Our Part 2 method (0-50 range): 3.46 ÷ 86.6 = 4% similarity
  • Unit vector method (if normalized): 0.28 ÷ 2 = 14% similarity
  • Cosine Similarity (from Part 1): 0.73 = 73% similarity

Same vectors, three different interpretations!

But here's the kicker: For unit vectors, L2 distance and cosine similarity produce identical rankings!

If vectors A and C are more similar than A and B using cosine similarity, they'll also be more similar using L2 distance. You get the exact same search results either way.

But Even This Has Drawbacks:

Even with unit vectors making normalization straightforward and rankings equivalent, operational complexity remains:

  • Need to verify your model outputs unit vectors
  • Mixed models in one system → different normalization rules
  • Model updates can change normalization assumptions

Alternative approaches for production:

  1. Fixed domain thresholds: Learn L2 ranges from initial dataset, keep stable
  2. Rolling window normalization: Use recent N documents for stability
  3. Just use Cosine Similarity: Self-normalizing, immune to dataset changes

This brings us full circle to my original post: For most RAG systems, Cosine Similarity wins not just because of semantic focus, but because of operational simplicity.

The final insight? L2 Distance normalization is mathematically sound and often feasible, but adds operational complexity. A score of 0.85 in Cosine Similarity means the same thing today, tomorrow, and after 10,000 new documents.

Choose based on your system's complexity tolerance and data characteristics, not just mathematical elegance.

 

Read the entire series here :

  • Part I - Why Cosine Similarity > L2 Distance for RAG Systems (usually) [Part 1/3]
  • Part II - How to Actually Interpret L2 Distance Results [Part 2/3]
  • Part III - The Production Reality: Why Cosine Similarity Often Wins Anyway [Part 3/3]
  •

entry.title: Reimagining SAP MDG Field Validations Low-Code Approach | UI Field Control Made Dynamic Yet Simple !
entry.content:
Overview

Working with SAP MDG (Master Data Governance) often means balancing two realities: robust governance and agile adaptability. One of the common we face during MDG implementation is managing field validations with user-friendly alerts.

Whether a field should be hidden, mandatory or optional often depends on multiple factors like CR type, entity, company code or user role etc. Traditionally, these validations are implemented via BRF+ expressions, Enhancements in feeder classes, Hard-coded logic in the UI layer/BAdIs.

The Problem: Code Dependency in Field Validation

MDG validations are crucial for maintaining clean and consistent master data. However, the standard methods come with limitations & most teams rely on:

  • Feeder class logic: Feeder class enhancements require ABAP expertise and testing cycles.
  • UI rule maintenance is scattered and often difficult to track.
  • BRF+ rules or hardcoded validations code: Every new business scenario (e.g. different behaviour based on company code or region) means new logic and testing.
  • No centralized view of how validations are controlled across the application.

- While these are functional, they’re rigid, developer-dependent, and non-scalable. This complexity makes it difficult for functional/ business teams to adapt validations quickly and every change creates a dependency on development teams reducing agility.

Challenges: Too Much Code, Too Little Agility
  • Hide/Show fields based on Role UI logic  is non-transparent.
  • Maintain field rules over time - Static logic -No visibility for business teams.
  • The key pain point: Every small validation change turns into a development cycle.

- But what if we could avoid coding and development cycle every time specially for simple level properties or validations, yet still provide fully dynamic, context-sensitive field behaviour.

Solution: Rule the Config-Driven Field Validation Framework [ All In One Place]

To address these challenges, a custom configuration table was designed to serve as a central validation engine covers easy level validations. Field behaviour across various MDG entities and scenarios was defined through this table eliminating the need for code changes every time.

- These rules are consumed at runtime ensuring the UI responds dynamically based on master data context.

- Validation Types : M = Mandatory, O = Optional, H = Hidden, R = Read Only

- This table is maintained via a simple maintenance view or SM30 transaction/T-Code assigned to TMG accessible to key users or functional consultants.

All this happens without modifying feeder classes or enhancing the UI every time.

Benefits of This Approach
  • No More Coding: New validation rules don’t require any ABAP changes at-least for simple validations.
  • Business Empowerment: Functional/Business teams can maintain rules via a user-friendly view.
  • Central Governance: One place to view and manage all field control rules along with by whom & when it’s added or changed.
  • Audit-friendly: clearly see why and when a rule applies.
  • No BRF+ overload: clean separation of decision logic.
  • Faster Change Cycles: No transports or regression testing required for every new validation rule.
  • Scalable to all MDG domains MDG – S,M,C (Supplier, Material, Customer)
Real Impact
  • After rollout: Reduced ~70% of validation-related change requests.
  • Built an enterprise-grade, reusable framework used across modules.
  • Delivered faster time-to-compliance for changing regulatory/market needs.
  • It's a shift from “write code to validate” ➝ to “configure to adapt”
How It Works: Plug-In Logic, Zero Enhancement for later changes
  1. Custom Table is maintained in SM30 or Z/Y T-code (or via custom UI).
  2. Lightweight enhancement logic is added once to retrieve rules based on runtime values.
  3. The UI behaviour adjusts: field becomes hidden, read-only mandatory or optional.
  4. No need to touch feeder classes again & ever.
You can even combine this with:
  • Workflow context (field is mandatory only in approval steps)
  • User or Role-level personalization (validation based on user group)
  • For multiple conditions can add different Sr. No conditions. E.g. Department is mandatory for Company Code =  ‘0001’  & '0005', So Can add two records with different Sr. No.
Requirement - For CR Type: ZCCT1P2: Create Cost Center with Hry. Assignments:
  1.    Business Area to be hidden.
  2.    Profit Center is mandatory.
  3.    Department is mandatory for Company Code =  ‘0001’
  4.    User Responsible is mandatory for cost center category = ‘%’.
Implementation

[ Fig. 1.0. Table Contents ]

  • ZCCT1P2: Create Cost Center with Hry. Assignments.
  • Step ‘0’ defines Requestor Level.
  • Tech Property  & Conditional Tech-Property stores technical attributes.
  • Table also contains audit columns to track changes.
  1. BAdI: USMD_ACC_FLD_PROP_CUST_DEP_SET
  • EI: ZFI_ES_MDG_FLD_PROP
  • BAdI Implementation: ZFI_MDG_FLD_PROP
  • Class: ZCL_FI_MDG_FLD_PROP
  • Method: IF_EX_USMD_ACC_FLD_PROP_CDS~MODIFY_FLD_PROP_ATTR
  METHOD if_ex_usmd_acc_fld_prop_cds~modify_fld_prop_attr.

    DATA:lr_model TYPE REF TO if_usmd_app_context.
    CALL METHOD cl_usmd_app_context=>get_context
      RECEIVING
        eo_context = lr_model.
    IF lr_model IS BOUND.
      CALL METHOD lr_model->get_attributes
        IMPORTING
          ev_crequest_type = DATA(lv_cr_type)
          ev_crequest_step = DATA(lv_cr_step).
    ENDIF.

    DATA: go_tab_descr   TYPE REF TO cl_abap_tabledescr,
          go_struc_descr TYPE REF TO cl_abap_structdescr,
          lt_components  TYPE abap_component_tab.
    DATA: name_range TYPE RANGE OF zfi_mdg_valt-condition_tech.

    "Pre-Exception Handling To Get Valid Fields For it_data As not-valid entry entered in Condition Property
    go_tab_descr ?= cl_abap_tabledescr=>describe_by_data( it_data ).
    CHECK sy-subrc = 0.
    go_struc_descr ?= go_tab_descr->get_table_line_type( ). "Get the structure of your internal table
    lt_components  = go_struc_descr->get_components( ).
    REFRESH name_range.
    name_range = VALUE #( FOR wa IN lt_components ( sign = 'I' option = 'EQ' low = wa-name ) ).

    "Check Mandatory(*)/Hide/Read Only/Optional Attribute Property
    SELECT * FROM zfi_mdg_valt INTO TABLE (it_mdg_val)
          WHERE cr_type = _cr_type AND cr_step = _cr_step AND entity_type = _entity
          AND zmodule = '0G' AND active = 'X'.
    IF sy-subrc = 0.

      LOOP AT ct_fld_prop ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_data_2>).
        ASSIGN COMPONENT 'USMD_FP' OF STRUCTURE <fs_data_2> TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_fld_prop_2>).

        LOOP AT it_mdg_val INTO DATA(wa_val) WHERE entity_type = iv_entity. "Traverse Custom Defined Properties

          IF wa_val-condition_tech IS NOT INITIAL. "Check If Condition maintained or free of any condition
            IF  wa_val-condition_tech IN name_range.    "Preventive Action
              READ TABLE it_data ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_data_con>)
              WITH KEY (wa_val-condition_tech) = wa_val-condition_val.
              "Condition is not matching, continue with next iteration of properties of it_mdg_val
              IF sy-subrc NE 0.
                CONTINUE.
              ENDIF.
            ELSE.
              CONTINUE.   "Not Valid Condition Attribute/Property
            ENDIF.
          ENDIF.

          "Check for further tech property
          ASSIGN COMPONENT wa_val-tech_name OF STRUCTURE <fs_fld_prop_2> TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_field_2>).
          IF <ls_field_2> IS ASSIGNED.
            <ls_field_2> = wa_val-property.
          ENDIF.
          UNASSIGN  <ls_field_2>.
        ENDLOOP.

        UNASSIGN <fs_fld_prop_2>.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.

  ENDMETHOD.
       2.  BAdI: USMD_RULE_SERVICE
  • EI: ZFI_ES_MDG_VAL
  • BAdI Implementation: ZFI_MDG_VAL
  • Class: ZCL_FI_MDG_VAL
  • Method: IF_EX_USMD_RULE_SERVICE~CHECK_ENTITY
  METHOD if_ex_usmd_rule_service~check_entity.

    DATA: lr_model   TYPE REF TO if_usmd_app_context.
    DATA: go_tab_descr   TYPE REF TO cl_abap_tabledescr,
          go_struc_descr TYPE REF TO cl_abap_structdescr,
          lt_components  TYPE abap_component_tab.
    DATA: name_range TYPE RANGE OF zfi_mdg_valt-condition_tech.

    CALL METHOD cl_usmd_app_context=>get_context
      RECEIVING
        eo_context = lr_model.
    IF lr_model IS BOUND.
      CALL METHOD lr_model->get_attributes
        IMPORTING
          ev_crequest_type = DATA(lv_cr_type)
          ev_crequest_step = DATA(lv_cr_step).
    ENDIF.

    "Pre-Exception Handling To Get Valid Fields For it_data As not-valid entry entered in Condition Property
    go_tab_descr ?= cl_abap_tabledescr=>describe_by_data( it_data ).
    CHECK sy-subrc = 0.
    go_struc_descr ?= go_tab_descr->get_table_line_type( ). "Get the structure of your internal table
    lt_components  = go_struc_descr->get_components( ).
    REFRESH name_range.
    name_range = VALUE #( FOR wa IN lt_components ( sign = 'I' option = 'EQ' low = wa-name ) ).

    "Mandatory Check Working for FI : GL,CC,PC
    SELECT * FROM zfi_mdg_valt INTO TABLE (it_zfi_mdg_valderv)
      WHERE cr_type = _cr_type AND cr_step = _cr_step
      AND zmodule = '0G' AND property = 'M' AND active = 'X'.

    LOOP AT it_zfi_mdg_valderv INTO DATA(wa_val).
      LOOP AT it_data ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_data>).
        IF <ls_data> IS ASSIGNED.

          IF wa_val-condition_tech IS NOT INITIAL. "Check If Condition maintained or free of any condition
            IF  wa_val-condition_tech IN name_range.    "Preventive Action

              ASSIGN COMPONENT wa_val-condition_tech OF STRUCTURE <ls_data> TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_con>).
              IF <ls_con> IS ASSIGNED AND <ls_con> IS NOT INITIAL.
                IF <ls_con> NE wa_val-condition_val.
                  CONTINUE.
                ENDIF.
                UNASSIGN <ls_con>.
              ENDIF.

            ELSE.
              APPEND VALUE #( msgty = 'E' msgv1 = 'Please check entity name' msgv2 = wa_val-condition_tech   ) TO et_message.
              CONTINUE.
              "msgid = 'zfi_mdg_message'  msgno = 000
            ENDIF.
          ENDIF.

          ASSIGN COMPONENT wa_val-tech_name OF STRUCTURE <ls_data> TO FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_value>).
          IF <ls_value> IS ASSIGNED AND <ls_value> IS INITIAL.
            APPEND VALUE #( msgid = wa_val-msgid msgty = wa_val-msgty
            msgno = wa_val-msgno msgv1 = wa_val-msgv1 msgv2 = wa_val-msgv2 ) TO et_message.
            UNASSIGN <ls_value>.
          ENDIF.
        ENDIF.

      ENDLOOP.
    ENDLOOP.


  ENDMETHOD.
Output
  1. Scenario I

       1.1. With Loading, Business area is hidden, Profit Center is mandatory, Department & User Responsible are not mandatory.

[ Fig 1.1 : Initial Screen ]

1.2.  With entering Company code = ‘0001’, Department becomes mandatory (*).

[ Fig 1.2 : Department is mandatory ]

2. Scenario II

2.1. For Company code = ‘0003’, Department is not mandatory

[ Fig 2.1 : Department not mandatory ]

3. Scenario III

3.1. With loading, User Responsible is not mandatory by default.

[ Fig 3.1 : User Responsible not mandatory ]

3.2. With Cost center category = ‘%’, User Responsible becomes mandatory.

[ Fig 3.2 : User Responsible mandatory ]

3.3. Click on Check: As Profit Center & User Responsible are blank, we got error messages defined in our Z table.

[ Fig 3.3 : Messages ]

3.4.Errors on Check

[ Fig 3.4 : Messages ]

4. Additional Sanity Check:

To troubleshoot scenario where user has maintained conditional-tech-property/attribute as not-valid property value ( By mistake ; ) So below error will appear on hitting Check.

As we are using this conditional-tech-property to read IT_DATA, it’s safe idea to check pre-hand whether the property is valid and actually present in IT_DATA to refrain from last moment surprises. (or technically a dump ; )

[ Fig. 4.1. Preventive Code ]

[ Fig : 4.2. conditional tech attribute = ‘CCODECCTR1’ is not valid attribute of data model ]

5. Message Class: Generic 000, So No transport required every time for new messages.

[ Fig : 5 Message Class ]

Conclusion

- SAP MDG is built to empower governed, agile master data. But flexibility shouldn’t mean fragility. With a configurable validation engine, we’ve transformed how we think about field behavior turning a developer-led task into a business-led control mechanism and maximum governance.

Thanks !

entry.title: Register Now: Unlock the Power of SAP HANA Cloud
entry.content:
Migrating from on-premise systems to SAP HANA Cloud is a strategic move towards embracing the future of Digital Transformation and data-driven innovation. SAP HANA Cloud equips your businesses with the tools to optimize every aspect of operations, from performance and scalability to analytics and security.

We invite you to an informative webinar. In it, we will share insights on how customers can successfully migrate their on-premise SAP HANA environments to the Cloud

During the sessions, customers will learn about:

  • The benefits of migrating to  SAP HANA Cloud

  • Step-by-step migration strategies and best practice

  • SAP Value Assurance: Our unique offering to support customers throughout their journey to SAP HANA Cloud

Session 1

📅 Date: June 25, 2025
⏰ Time: 10:00 AM – 11:00 AM ET
🌐 Location: Online 

Session 2

📅 Date: June 26, 2025
⏰ Time: 8:00 AM – 11:00 AM ET
🌐 Location: Online 

Register Now

 

SAP HANA Cloud Migration Office Hours: Ask the Experts

Following the insightful webinar Unlock the Power of SAP HANA Cloud: Migration Approaches, we are excited to invite you to our office hours session designed exclusively for further discussions and Q&A. 

Check out the registration page.

Start your journey with SAP HANA Cloud today

Learn about SAP HANA Cloud: comprehensive information about the product including technical information, Features, how to empower developers to build intelligent apps, to take an interactive product tour, and to see how customers are succeeding with SAP HANA Cloud. 
The SAP Value Assurance service is designed to ensure that your migration not only happens smoothly but also delivers tangible business value. Key Objectives & Main Deliverables 
SAP Community Blog posts is a vibrant online platform where SAP professionals and partners share knowledge, insights, and experiences related to SAP technologies, products, and services. The community serves as a hub for learning, collaboration, offering a wealth of resources for individuals across various roles and industries. 

Customer Stories - SAP HANA Cloud Customer Stories Finder

Further Details about SAP HANA Cloud migration scenarios

SAP HANA Cloud is a powerful, cloud-based data platform that combines database and application development capabilities with strong analytics and intelligence. It offers a comprehensive suite of tools designed to handle large volumes of data, providing real-time insights and supporting complex analytics. Leveraging the cloud, SAP HANA Cloud offers unparalleled flexibility, scalability, and robustness, making it an ideal solution for businesses of all sizes looking to optimize their data processes.
Whether you're migrating your on-premise SAP HANA environment or planning a new implementation, the cloud can be a game-changer, offering improved performance, scalability, and cost efficiencies. SAP provides multiple options to facilitate this migration, ensuring a smooth transition that suits your organization’s needs.

It's important to note that not every HANA implementation is suitable for migration to SAP HANA Cloud. Appropriate on-premise implementation scenarios for SAP HANA Cloud migration may include Sidecar scenarios, DataMart scenarios, or custom-built applications running on local servers, which often have specific data storage and processing requirements.
For each scenario, consider the specific business requirements, data sources, and integration points. SAP provides  various tools and services, to facilitate these implementation scenarios. 

Of course manual data migration can be done using all kinds of data integration tools and in these cases data mapping needs to be done automatically, but for simplicity reasons, this blog covers the scenarios outlined in the HANA cloud migration guide here.

This blog explores three options to achieve the migration to SAP HANA Cloud. The approaches include engaging the SAP Migration Factory for smooth transitions, utilizing the SAP Value Assurance package, which can enhance migration efforts and lastly, the Self-Service Migration for SAP HANA Cloud Tool to complete the migration:

1) SAP Migration Factory

The SAP Migration Factory approach offers a hands-on, expert-guided migration service. It is well-suited for enterprises seeking a structured and comprehensive method to move their SAP HANA workloads to the cloud. This program encompasses assessments, automation tools, accelerators, and industry best-practices from SAP and our partners. The goal of the Migration Factory approach is to streamline the entire migration journey from initial planning to go-live to achieve a faster path to productivity and adoption.
See how the SAP Migration Factory for SAP BTP, a high-touch consulting program, can put you on the fast track toward realizing the advantages of SAP HANA Cloud. Expert guidance services are delivered remotely by seasoned teams from SAP or approved SAP partners. Download the Document or visit the link below. 

Migration Factory: https://www.sap.com/documents/2024/08/c637a2fb-d07e-0010-bca6-c68f7e60039b.html

2) SAP Value Assurance

By engaging with SAP Value Assurance, you gain access to a comprehensive suite of services, support, and expertise tailored to your specific needs, enabling you to plan and execute a successful move to SAP HANA Cloud.
SAP Value Assurance is designed to ensure that your migration not only happens smoothly but also delivers tangible business value. Key Objectives and Main Deliverables are 

  • The primary objective of the SAP Value Assurance Package for SAP HANA Cloud is to provide the customer a quick turnaround to realize value with SAP HANA Cloud in all phases of a project.

  • A Focus TQM will be assigned who guides the customer through the deliveries, acts as a single point of contact for the customer and for other SAP units.

  • All SAP Value Assurance services are available; however, the key focus is around migration, innovation and clean-core realization.

  • The deliverables will be in line with the SAP Value Assurance Package standards.

  • Minimum number of service days are 50 including a Focus TQM.

3) Self-service migration tool for SAP HANA Cloud

The self-service migration tool for SAP HANA Cloud streamlines and automates key migration tasks, such as catalog and data migration. Accessible via the SAP BTP cockpit, this tool features a user-friendly migration wizard that guides users step-by-step through the migration process. It assesses the compatibility of your current database with SAP HANA Cloud, identifying which features, database objects, and SQL statements can be transitioned and flagging those that require adaptation.
By addressing potential issues before the migration starts, the tool reduces uncertainties and ensures a smoother transition. This makes it an efficient and cost-effective option for various migration scenarios, including migrations from SAP HANA service for BTP in both Cloud Foundry and Neo environments, as well as from on-premises SAP HANA implementations. Additionally, the tool provides secure temporary storage for your data throughout the migration at no extra cost.

SAP HANA Cloud Migration: https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud/sap-hana-cloud-migration-guide/sap-hana-cloud-migration-guide
